Received: 13 August 2023    | Revised: 5 April 2024 | Accepted: 10 April 2024
DOI: 10.1111/jvs.13259

                                                                                               Journal of Vegetation Science
RESEARCH ARTICLE




Trait divergence in plant community assembly is generated by
environmental factor interactions

Valério D. Pillar

Department of Ecology and Centre of
Ecology, Universidade Federal do Rio            Abstract
Grande do Sul, Porto Alegre, RS, Brazil
                                                Question: What conditions drive trait divergence during community assembly
Correspondence                                  through environmental filtering, and why are some communities more trait-­diverse
Valério D. Pillar, Department of Ecology
                                                than others?
and Centre of Ecology, Universidade
Federal do Rio Grande do Sul, Porto             Methods: An individual-­
                                                                       based, stochastic, spatially explicit metacommunity
Alegre, RS, Brazil.
                                                simulation model produced data on species traits, spatially autocorrelated, nested,
Email: vpillar@ufrgs.br
                                                feedback-­generated environmental factors, and resulting community composition. I
Funding information
                                                quantified environmentally driven alpha trait divergence using the correlation r(RE) to
Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento
Científico e Tecnológico (CNPq), Grant/         measure the relationship between Rao functional diversity and environmental factors.
Award Number: 307689/2014-­0,
                                                Environmentally driven beta trait divergence was assessed through the correlation
431193/2016-­9, 313315/2022-­1 and
457531/2012-­6                                  r(VE), involving environmental factors and the squared residuals (V) of a second-­order
                                                polynomial regression of community-­weighted means on environmental factors (E).
Co-­ordinating Editor: Zoltán Botta-­Dukát
                                                Permutation tests, assuming independence between traits and species composition,
                                                were used to establish the significance of r(RE) and r(VE). Additionally, the method
                                                was applied to grassland and soil data collected in plots across southern Brazil. Both
                                                simulated and real data were analysed at two spatial resolutions.
                                                Results: Significant r(VE) correlations were frequent with factor interactions
                                                incorporated in community assembly simulations, while r(VE) correlations mostly
                                                remained within expected Type I error range when factor interactions were absent.
                                                r(VE) was stronger than r(RE) at a finer spatial resolution and weaker than r(RE) when
                                                smaller community units were combined into larger units. r(VE) for specific leaf area
                                                (SLA) was related to soil variables, likely due to their interacting effects with total
                                                vegetation cover. When small plots were aggregated into larger units, r(VE) became
                                                non-­significant, while r(RE) increased.
                                                Conclusions: Environmentally driven trait divergence emerges during community
                                                assembly due to interactions between factors affecting the selection of individuals
                                                based on their traits. When the effects of these factors are spatially nested, including
                                                hidden, feedback-­
                                                                 generated ones, trait divergence arises at the beta or alpha
                                                dimension, depending on the scale of the community units. This suggests that plant-­
                                                to-­plant positive or negative interactions, which can feedback on environmental
                                                factors and generate heterogeneity, do not necessarily lead to trait divergence if
                                                these factors do not interact.



© 2024 International Association for Vegetation Science.

J Veg Sci. 2024;35:e13259.                                                  ﻿                        wileyonlinelibrary.com/journal/jvs   | 1 of 14
https://doi.org/10.1111/jvs.13259
          | Journal of Vegetation Science

                                                KEYWORDS
                                                community assembly, environmental filtering, environmentally driven trait divergence,
                                                factor interaction, feedback-­generated factors, grassland, metacommunity, plant community,
                                                simulation model




1    |    I NTRO D U C TI O N                                                 and reassembly (Pillar et al., 2024). Additionally, environmental fac-
                                                                              tors altered by these plant-­to-­plant interactions might remain hidden
As vegetation scientists, we analyse the spatial and temporal ar-             and not readily observable when they affect community assembly
rangement of plants to identify patterns of plant species in com-             at a finer scale than the resolution of the studied community units
munities and the associated environmental factors. These factors              (Pillar et al., 2021).
may include variables reflecting available resources, site condi-                 An intriguing pattern observed in trait-­based community anal-
tions, and disturbances within community sites. Additionally, we              ysis is the presence of trended residuals around the predicted
investigate how plant communities impact ecosystem functions                  response when using linear models to CWM trait values to envi-
and trophic levels. Despite the importance of predicting patterns,            ronmental factors (e.g., figure 4b,c in Bruelheide et al., 2018; fig-
linking them to underlying processes — often unobservable — is                ures 2d and 4d in Laughlin et al., 2018). This suggests that the trait
challenging and can only be inferred. Knowledge of plant traits               may also be associated with other, hidden environmental factor(s)
has become crucial in this context. Traits often exhibit conver-              interacting with the environmental gradient under consideration,
gence patterns at the community level (e.g., Carlucci et al., 2012;           implying that communities apparently under similar environmental
Bruelheide et al., 2018), making the filtering analogy appealing              conditions may exhibit distinct CWM trait values. Consequently,
for explaining community assembly. According to this analogy,                 the magnitude of the trait residuals, whether positive or negative
plants with unsuitable trait values for local conditions are filtered         around the predicted CWM trait values, may respond to the en-
out (Keddy, 1992; Hillerislambers et al., 2012; Kraft et al., 2015).          vironmental factor that defines the gradient. Here, I define such
Consequently, the stronger the filtering effect of an environmental           trended trait variation in the residuals, related to environmental
factor, the more similar the selected plants within one community             factors, as environmentally driven beta trait divergence, and I define
will become (de Bello et al., 2009). Furthermore, changes between             the trait variation within communities related to environmental
communities, in terms of community-­weighted means (CWM), may                 factors as environmentally driven alpha trait divergence. However, for
be related to gradients defined by this filtering environmental fac-          brevity, I may henceforth refer to these simply as beta trait diver-
tor (Pillar et al., 2009). However, we also observe that plants coex-         gence and alpha trait divergence. The challenge lies in understand-
isting within one community may diverge in their trait values, and            ing how such trait divergence can arise in community assembly and
the magnitude of such divergence may be related to environmen-                how it may be perceived within or between communities depend-
tal factors (e.g. Carlucci et al., 2012; Carboni et al., 2014), raising       ing on the unit scale.
the questions of whether trait divergence in community assembly                   It can be demonstrated that trait divergence can be generated by
could arise from environmental filtering and why some communi-                environmental filtering during community assembly. Figure 1 pres-
ties are more trait-­diverse than others.                                     ents simple cases of hypothetical scenarios illustrating the expected
    The prevailing theory explaining trait divergence is that com-            values of a trait (t1) when species varying in the values of that trait
petition is avoided through niche differentiation (Chesson, 2000;             are selected (filtered) by two environmental factors (e1 and e2), ei-
Mayfield & Levine, 2010). According to coexistence theory, we                 ther acting additively or interacting. Let us assume that only factor
would expect species assembled in communities to be more distinct             e1 can be measured, while factor e2 is hidden and not measurable.
from each other in terms of niche-­relevant traits (i.e., limiting similar-   Thus, only factor e1 can be used for the analysis of trait–environ-
ity) compared to those randomly selected from the regional pool, es-          ment relationships. When the factors do not interact with each
pecially when there are strong fitness inequalities between species           other in terms of their selection effects on the trait (Figure 1a), the
(Chesson, 2000; Mayfield & Levine, 2010). Although some empirical             analysed residuals simply add noise to the prediction of t1 based
evidence supports limiting similarity (e.g. Stubbs & Wilson, 2004;            on e1 and are not related to factor e1. A similar pattern of random
Wilson, 2007), the link between limiting similarity and community             noise is observed when trait t1 is selected by hidden factor e2 only
assembly remains elusive (e.g. Price & Pärtel, 2013). In any case,            (Appendix S1).
such trait divergence may vary between communities (de Bello                      Moving to a more complex scenario, Figure 1b, inspired by Cade
et al., 2009; Carboni et al., 2014). However, interactions between            and Noon (2003), illustrates the expected trait values for trait t1
plants are often mediated by various environmental factors that in-           as a function of both factors e1 and e2 and their interaction. The
teracting plants modify at fine spatial and/or temporal scales. These         interaction between factors can modify the strength and direction
altered, feedback-­generated environmental factors serve as select-           of one factor's effect based on the other factor. Consequently, this
ing filters throughout the dynamic process of community assembly              can impact the variation of expected trait values across communities

F I G U R E 1 Hypothetical examples
illustrating expected values of a trait
(t1) in a set of simulated communities.
The trait is selected (filtered) by two
random environmental factors (e1 and e2)
according to specified linear functions. In
(a), trait t1 is additively affected by factors
e1 and e2, and in (b), it is determined by
both factors plus their interaction. The
linear function slopes are shown in the
insets. The red trendline represents the
response of trait t1 modelled a posteriori
with the generated data, using only factor
e1 as a predictor and ignoring factor e2
and the interaction (greyed in the insets),
as if e2 were not measured. Note that
the effect of simulated values of factor
e2 on trait t1 was nested within the
nesting factor e1. For this, each subset
of 10 random values of e2 in subunits
corresponded to one identical random
value of e1 defining a nesting unit. That is,
larger nesting units contained 10 subunits
that did not vary for e1 but did for e2.
Random values were normally distributed.
The corresponding variance of trait t1
within each of these nesting units and its
observed response to factor e1 are shown
in graphs c,d.




that share similar environmental conditions given by factor e1 but          spatially nested in their effects (see Figure S1f in Appendix S1 for
vary regarding hidden factor e2. A similar funnel-­shaped pattern is        a case in which the factors are not nested). Such beta and alpha
observed when t1 is selected only by the interaction of the two fac-        trait divergences are interconnected and scale-­dependent when
tors (Appendix S1). In the community assembly process, the hidden           the interacting factors are spatially nested, highlighting the need
factor modulates the selection effects of factor e1 and, vice versa,        to assess them at defined spatial grains (analogously to Carboni
variation in factor e1 modulates the effect of environmental het-           et al., 2014).

pattern of trait divergence in the residuals that is associated with        investigate patterns of trait divergence in a metacommunity and
factor e1.                                                                  gain a better understanding of why some plant communities are

spatially nested in their effects on the expected values of a trait,        tion model, I demonstrate that trait divergence patterns, both at the
what is perceived as trait divergence between communities (beta             alpha and beta dimensions, can emerge from environmental filtering
trait divergence) at finer scales becomes trait divergence within           driven by factors that are often hidden and feedback-­generated, and
communities (alpha trait divergence) when observed at coarser               that interact in their effects on plant traits. Additionally, I describe
scales. In the case of non-­interacting factors (Figure 1c), trait varia-   a novel method to measure and test beta trait divergence, and as-
tion within the larger units is unrelated to factor e1. However, when       sess the accuracy and power of both beta and alpha trait divergence
the factors interact (Figure 1d), trait variation within the larger         using simulated data. Finally, I apply this method to analyse grass-
units is affected by factor e1, provided the interacting factors are        land vegetation data.

2    |    M E TH O D S                                                   normalized W by B yielded matrix T containing the CWM trait values
                                                                         of the sites. This matrix operation is equivalent to the well-­known
2.1 | Measuring environmentally driven trait                             equation for computation of CWMs using scalar operations (see e.g.
divergence                                                               de Bello et al., 2021).
                                                                            To measure environmentally driven beta trait divergence, a linear
I followed the steps outlined below, visually represented in             regression was fitted for each trait j on one or more environmental
Figure 2a. Matrix W characterizes community sites based on species       factors described in matrix E as predictors and the tij values as re-
composition, while matrix B describes species based on their             sponse variable. Then, the squared residual vij2 of each community i
traits. Normalizing matrix W by unit site totals and multiplying the     and trait j was computed as




F I G U R E 2 General framework for measuring environmentally driven beta and alpha trait divergence (a) and the corresponding
permutation tests (b). In (a), the method for beta trait divergence begins with the computation of trait means at the community level
(CWMs in matrix T), which are then regressed against factors in E. For each community i and trait j, v2ij is the squared deviation between
the observed CWM and the predicted CWM trait value based on factors in E. For simplicity, a simple linear regression is depicted, but a
polynomial linear regression of second order should be used by including the squared factor values as additional columns in E, as actual
CWM trait responses often exhibit a humpbacked shape. In matrix notation, this is expressed as V = [T − E(E'E)−1E'T]°2, where °2 indicates
that such residuals are squared element-­wise. The matrix correlation between V and E measures environmentally driven beta trait
divergence. In the depicted case, the residuals between observed and predicted trait values increase with the increase of factor e1. Note
that V may be a vector when just one trait is considered, or may be a matrix when the v2ij values are computed for more than one trait taken
alone. Additionally, the correlation between Rao entropy in vector R and E measures environmentally driven alpha trait divergence. In (b),
the rows (species) of trait matrix B are permuted, and the derived measures [T0, R0, V0, r(R0 E) and r(V0 E)] are recomputed, keeping species
composition matrix W and environmental matrix E constant. After many iterations, the probabilities of finding r(VE) values under the null
model are calculated as the frequency of permutation iterations in which r(V0 E) were not smaller than observed r(VE), divided by the number
of iterations. The same permutation procedure is used for r(RE).
                                                                                                                                             | 5 of 14




                                                                                                                                                          16541103, 2024, 3, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/jvs.13259 by Capes, Wiley Online Library on [05/02/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License
PILLAR
                                                                                                            Journal of Vegetation Science

predicted by one or more environmental factors. It can be demon-             (Figure 2b). The correlation r(RE) could be based on CWV, which is
strated that vij2 is equivalent to the difference between community-­        equivalent to Rao functional diversity computed using squared trait
weighted variance (CWV, Enquist et al., 2015) relative to ̂t ij and          distances between species (Champely & Chessel, 2002). Other func-
relative to tij (Appendix S2). To proceed with the analysis, the vij2 val-   tional diversity metrics within communities could also be used (e.g.
ues were compiled into matrix V. More directly, matrix V can be com-         de Bello et al., 2021).

puted as V = [T − E(E'E) E'T]° , where ° indicates that such residuals
are element-­wise squared. For simplicity, the method is illustrated in
Figure 2a by a simple linear regression of the CWM trait response on         2.2 | Type I error and power
the environmental factor(s). However, this assumption may not hold
true, particularly when the CWM trait values exhibit a non-­linear re-       Is the test of the correlations r(VE) and r(RE) for measuring trait di-
sponse, such as a humpbacked one. Therefore, to avoid possible mis-          vergence reliable? To address this question, I used a stochastic,
specification of the model, a second-­order polynomial regression was        individual-­based, spatially explicit metacommunity simulation model
used in the analyses.                                                        (Pillar et al., 2021), which was expanded to include environmental fac-
   Beta trait divergence was measured by calculating the correla-            tors capable of being feedback-­generated. The objective was to as-
tion r(VE) between matrices V and E, as illustrated in Figure 2a.            sess the accuracy (Type I error) of the test in correctly identifying the
Since multiple traits and environmental factors can be involved,             absence of trait divergence when the factors were not interacting,
r(VE) was defined as a matrix correlation. In this case, the Rd cor-         and its power to detect trait divergence when it was expected due to
relation (Omelka & Hudecová, 2013) was adopted. The Rd correla-              the interaction of driving factors in the selection of species based on
tion is analogous to a Mantel correlation, but instead of the original       their traits. Multiple data sets were simulated, some with expectedly
distances, it is computed using the full matrix of Gower-­centred dis-       significant correlations r(VE) and r(RE), and others without.
tances. According to Omelka and Hudecová (2013), the Rd is advan-               The metacommunity assembly model is rooted in the environ-
tageous in terms of power over the Mantel correlation, and in terms          mental filtering analogy. In trait space, sites are described by ex-
of computation demand compared to the PROTEST (Peres-­Neto &                 pected trait values at the community level, while species within a
Jackson, 2001) or the RV coefficient (Escoufier, 1973).                      given species pool are described by their trait values (Figure 3a).
   The significance of the correlation r(VE) was assessed through a          Expected trait values at the community level are determined by hy-
permutation approach under the null model assuming that the traits           pothetical functions describing trait–environmental relationships.
are unrelated to the species composition (Figure 2b). This involved          Assuming that propagules arrive at a site, the probability of individu-
permutation among the rows (species) of trait matrix B while keeping         als of a particular species becoming part of the plant community de-
species composition matrix W constant. The T, V, and r(VE) values            pends on two elements in this trait space, as explained in Figure 3b:
                                                                0
were recomputed for each permutation, yielding a new r(V E). This            (1) the importance of the trait for the species’ fitness, determined
process allowed testing the observed r(VE) against the distribution          by the centre and slope parameters of a logistic function, and (2) the
of r(V0 E) values generated from the permutations. The probabil-             proximity of the species’ trait value to the expected trait value at
ity (p-­value) of finding equally extreme r(VE) values under the null        the site. Further details on the metacommunity simulation model are
                                                                      0
model was determined by the proportion of iterations in which r(V E)         provided in Appendix S3. Each metacommunity data set was anal-
was larger than the observed r(VE). This permutation approach fol-           ysed to evaluate alpha and beta trait divergence using the simulated
lows a similar methodology to testing other trait-­based correlations        matrices of species composition, species traits and environmental
measured at the community level (Pillar et al., 2009, 2021; Pillar &         factors.
Duarte, 2010) and addresses the concerns raised by Zelený (2018).               To assess Type I error and power, two metacommunity simula-
Moreover, by keeping the species composition matrix W constant               tion scenarios were considered. These scenarios are hypothetical
across permutations, any spatial or temporal autocorrelation present         but constructed to resemble real cases. In scenario A, data simu-
in the composition data becomes incorporated into the null model.            lation was conducted using a species pool comprising 150 species
This design ensures that the accuracy of the test remains robust             described by three traits (t1, t2, and tn). The traits were uncor-
against spatial or temporal autocorrelation.                                 related, and each displayed independent responses to environmen-
   Along with the p-­values, a standardized effect size (SES) was            tal factors. Although traits in real data may be correlated with each
computed based on the mean and standard deviation (s.d.) of the              other at the species level (Díaz et al., 2016; Joswig et al., 2022), these

6 of 14                                                                                                                                     PILLAR


                                                                                                      F I G U R E 3 Example of a simulated trait
                                                                                                      space with nine sites, each described by
                                                                                                      expected trait values at the community
                                                                                                      level. Additionally, a pool of species (A–H)
                                                                                                      is mapped into the trait space based on
                                                                                                      their trait values. The expected trait
                                                                                                      values at each site were determined
                                                                                                      through linear functions representing
                                                                                                      trait responses to environment factors,
                                                                                                      similar to Figure 1. Both (a) and (b)
                                                                                                      depict the same trait space, but in (b),
                                                                                                      the modelling framework for trait-­based
                                                                                                      species selection (filtering) in community
                                                                                                      assembly simulation is illustrated for one
                                                                                                      site and two species within the same
                                                                                                      trait space. The probability (psurv) that
                                                                                                      an individual of a given species (A or B),
                                                                                                      after dispersal, will become part of the
                                                                                                      plant community at one site is the product
                                                                                                      of the psurv for the different traits. This
                                                                                                      depends on how critical the trait is for the
                                                                                                      species' fitness (defined by the centre and
                                                                                                      slope parameters of the logistic function)
                                                                                                      and how close it is (distance dhij in trait
                                                                                                      space for species h) to the expected
                                                                                                      community-­weighted mean (CWM) value
                                                                                                      trait j value at site i.

in the simulation parameters, trait divergence was anticipated in          250-­m long contour transects were marked, and 10 plots of 1 m2
the generated data. The linear coefficients (slopes) for the effect of     were surveyed within each transect. Soil variables were described
e1 on t1 ranged from zero to 0.6, and the effect of the interaction        at the transect level, while variables such as total vegetation cover,
e1 × e2 on t1 also ranged from zero to 0.6. The effect of e2 on trait      dung cover and rock cover were estimated at the plot level. The plant
t2 was set at −0.3, while trait tn remained neutral with respect to        traits measured for the species included specific leaf area (SLA), leaf
the factors and independent from the other traits. The maximum             area (LA), leaf dry matter content (LDMC), and leaf nitrogen (LeafN),
community size at each site was capped at 100 individuals, and each        with missing trait values imputed (see Hoss et al., 2022 for details).
simulation ran for 100 years with 50 colonization/extinction trials        The trait data were natural log-­transformed prior to analysis. Further
occurring at each community site per year.                                 information on the sampling design can be found in Appendix S5.
   For each combination of these parameters, 100 independent data              Initially, I analysed the data to identify the functional traits
sets, each comprising a simulated metacommunity, were analysed to          that best predicted community patterns without knowledge of
determine the p-­values for alpha and beta trait divergence measured       the underlying drivers. I employed the method proposed by Pillar
by r(RE) and r(VE), respectively. To reflect a situation in which only     et al. (2021), which calculates the Rd matrix correlation (Omelka
factor e1 was known and the other factor (e2) was unknown (hid-            & Hudecová, 2013) between two matrices: X, representing a trait-­
den), both r(RE) and r(VE) were computed considering only factor           based, fuzzy-­
                                                                                        weighted species composition (Pillar et al., 2009),
e1. The analyses were conducted at two spatial scales: one at a fine       and Y, representing a Beals-­
                                                                                                       transformed species composition
resolution using the 1296 simulated communities, and another at a          (Beals, 1984). Fuzzy weighting is a trait-­based transformation that
coarse resolution using 81 communities defined by pooling adjacent         fully captures the potential community composition in terms of
communities in a 4 × 4 configuration of rows and columns within the        traits (Pillar et al., 2009), and it can also be phylogenetically based
metacommunity grid. The analysis at the two spatial scales aimed           (Pillar & Duarte, 2010; Duarte et al., 2016). The resulting correlation
to assess whether the pooling of community units in such a nested          r(XY) quantifies the extent to which the traits used for fuzzy weight-
spatial structure would increase the power to detect alpha trait di-       ing reflect the species co-­occurrence patterns in Y. Subsequently,
vergence [r(RE)], while decreasing the power to detect beta trait          the traits that yielded the highest correlation r(XY) were used to test
divergence [r(VE)]. The proportion of significant tests at an alpha        for alpha and beta trait divergence ([r(RE) and r(VE)] in the grassland
threshold of 0.05 indicated the Type I error (in the absence of an         data. The analysis was done at two spatial scales: one at the fine
interaction effect) and power (in the presence of a non-­zero interac-     resolution of the 990 plots, and another at the coarse resolution by
tion effect specified in the simulation). For further details regarding    pooling the plots within each 250-­m transect.
the simulation parameters, please refer to Appendix S4. In a slight            Principal Components Analysis (PCA) was applied to summarize
variation of scenario A, a reduced set of simulations also included a      the soil variables. The first two principal axes were retained, which
squared effect of e1 on t1, which was set at −0.3. This was used to        accounted for 35.6% and 28.6% of the total standardized variance,
empirically test the method's sensitivity to model misspecification in     respectively. The first axis primarily represented soil aluminium,
measuring beta trait divergence, as it is expected that t1 will exhibit    while the second axis reflected soil calcium. These two synthetic
a strong humpbacked response to e1 in this case.                           variables, referred to as soilPC_Al and soilPC_Ca, were used in matrix
   Scenario B closely mirrored scenario A in terms of specified sim-       E to compute the correlations r(VE) and r(RE). To visualize the com-
ulation parameters, except for the inclusion of a feedback effect          positional patterns, PCA based on Euclidean distances was applied
from the developing communities to environmental factor e2. In             to the Beals-­transformed species composition matrix Y of plots by
this case, the initial values of factor e2 were set to zero and were       species.
subsequently influenced by the effect of the CWM values for trait
t1, which had a fixed linear coefficient of −0.3 on factor e2. As the
simulated metacommunity evolved, the values of factor e2 were ad-          3   |      R E S U LT S
justed by the feedback effect, and the interaction values (e1 × e2)
were recalculated.                                                         3.1 | Type I error and power

                                                                           The results for simulation scenarios A (Figure 4) and B (Figure 5)
2.3 | Analysis of grassland data                                           were partially similar regarding Type I error and power. While in
                                                                           scenario A the spatially autocorrelated, nested environmental
To test the method using real data, I analysed a data set comprising       structure was predetermined in its effects on traits and remained
990 grassland plots collected in south Brazil. The data set included       unchanged throughout the simulation process, in the more complex
information on plant species’ presence/absence and environmen-             scenario B the nested structure was produced by the evolving plant
tal variables (Menezes et al., 2022), as well as plant traits (Hoss        communities.
et al., 2022). The sampling design followed a nested structure, with           The analysis of simulated data under scenario A, at both fine and
11 sites of 5 × 5 km selected at the regional scale to represent various   coarse grains, showed that beta trait divergence (Figure 4a) was sel-
grassland types across six degrees of latitude. Within each site, nine     dom detected when there was no interaction between factors e1

and e2 incorporated into the environmental filtering process during        increased main effect of factor e1 on trait t1, Type I error was in-
community assembly. In the absence of factor interaction, the pro-         flated, particularly for trait t2. As expected, the neutral trait did not
portion of tests detecting beta trait divergence for trait t1 was low      exhibit any divergence.
and close to the designated alpha threshold for Type I error, as ex-          Regarding alpha trait divergence measured by r(RE) (Figure 5b),
pected, except for the cases with a higher main effect of factor e1 on     the results indicate low Type I error, especially in the coarse-­
trait t1, which exhibited some Type I error inflation. Moreover, trait     grained analyses. Furthermore, power was higher at the coarse-­
t2 and the neutral trait did not exhibit any divergence, as the fac-       grained level but decreased with an increasing main effect of
tor interaction had no influence on the simulation of community as-        factor e1 on trait t1.
sembly for these traits. Appendix S6 provides illustrative plots with
cases of data generated for different combinations of parameters.

tion r(VE) mostly accurately indicated the absence of beta trait
divergence when it was not anticipated in the simulated data. In           From the analysis, it was found that SLA [r(XY) = 0.346, p = 0.02,

on trait t1.                                                               while also showing higher Al content in the soil. This indicates the

                                                                                                                                               16541103, 2024, 3, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/jvs.13259 by Capes, Wiley Online Library on [05/02/2026]. PILLAR
                                                                                                   Journal of Vegetation Science
F I G U R E 4 Environmentally driven
trait divergence detected in simulated
metacommunity data considering the
specified combinations of parameters
under scenario A. Each graph in (a) shows
the proportions of significant beta trait
divergence measured by the correlation
r(VE) with increasing linear coefficients
for the effect of the interaction between
environmental factors e1 and e2 on trait t1
built into the simulation. The same is shown
in (b) for alpha trait divergence measured
by the correlation r(RE). As shown in (c), the
columns refer to the effect of the factor
e1 on trait t1 (ranging from 0 to 0.6). The
rows refer to the scale of the community
units, whether the analysis considered
the 1296 communities without pooling,
or with adjacent communities pooled into
81 communities. The other factor effects
were constant in every simulation. Trait
tn involved no selection effects. The red
lines correspond to trait t1, and the other
lines to traits t2 and tn. Trait divergence
is not expected in the absence of factor
interaction, thus the proportion of significant
trait divergence indicates Type I error, while
for non-­zero interaction it indicates the
power of the permutation test for r(VE) and
r(RE). Each proportion was found after 100
simulations for each specified combination of
parameters.




[r(RE) = 0.377, p = 0.041, SES = 2.19] compared to the scale of 1-­   4   |   DISCUSSION
 2
m plots [r(RE) = 0.176, p = 0.025, SES = 2.67]. In this case, r(RE)
represents the Rd correlation between Rao entropy (a vector)          The results obtained from simulated data provide compelling evi-
and the environmental matrix with the two factors (SoilPC_Al and      dence that certain plant communities within a metacommunity ex-
SoilPC_Ca).                                                           hibit higher trait diversity due to interactions between environmental
           | Journal of Vegetation Science




                                                                                                                                                    16541103, 2024, 3, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/jvs.13259 by Capes, Wiley Online Library on [05/02/2026]. 10 of 14                                                                                                                                   PILLAR




F I G U R E 5 Environmentally driven trait divergence detected in simulated metacommunity data, considering the specified combinations
of parameters under scenario B. Please refer to the caption of Figure 4, since the same explanations apply, also for the panels (a), (b) and (c).
However, in scenario B, the nested structure was created by the evolving plant communities. As indicated in (c), the linear coefficient of the
feedback effect of the community-­weighted mean (CWM) of trait t1 on factor e2 was set to 0.3, which modified e2 as plant communities
developed in the simulation process. In (c), factors and traits were translated into possible real ones representing a plausible path model
taking place in grassland communities under grazing. SLA, specific leaf area.
                                                                                                                                         | 11 of 14




                                                                                                                                                      16541103, 2024, 3, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/jvs.13259 by Capes, Wiley Online Library on [05/02/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License
PILLAR
                                                                                                        Journal of Vegetation Science
F I G U R E 6 Interaction plots relating to
specific leaf area (SLA) at the community
level (CWM_SLA) (a) the first (SoilPC_Al)
and (b) the second (SoilPC_Ca) principal
component of the soil data, using total
vegetation cover (VegCover) as the
variable interacting with the soil variable.
Note that the negative effect of variables
related to soil aluminium on SLA was
stronger under low total vegetation
cover (the light blue dashed line), and the
interaction effect SoilPC_Al × VegCover
was significant. Also, the effect of the
variables related to soil calcium on SLA
was stronger under high total vegetation
cover (the solid line).




factors influencing the selection of plants based on their traits. This    limiting for each other, nurse plants add resources, or improve con-
phenomenon is here identified as environmentally driven trait diver-       ditions, or provide anti-­herbivory protection that may benefit neigh-
gence. Furthermore, when these factors are spatially nested in their       bours. Shorter, palatable plants attract grazers to the same feeding
effects on traits, including hidden, feedback-­generated factors, such     station, which may also include not so palatable, taller plants, poten-
trait divergence can arise between or within communities, depend-          tially reducing competition for light. Additionally, allelopathic plants
ing on the scale of the community units. It is important to consider       release substances that could hinder the growth of neighbouring
that the filtering factors influencing species composition based on        plants. It is worth noting that some environmental factors, partic-
traits are not solely external to the community, such as coarse-­scale     ularly those modified in these plant-­to-­plant interactions, remain
climatic conditions. They also operate at a very fine scale, which         hidden and not directly observable. This is often the case when they
may reflect plant-­to-­plant interactions and their feedback on local      are linked to unknown past conditions or when they impact commu-
resources, conditions and disturbances in the direct neighbourhood         nity assembly at a finer resolution than the grain size of the studied
of single individual plants, thereby enhancing environmental hetero-       community units (Pillar et al., 2021). Hence, trait divergence may
geneity at the scale of microsites (Hillerislambers et al., 2012; Jiang    arise from the interactions between observable and such fine-­scale
& DeAngelis, 2013; Pillar et al., 2013, 2024).                             hidden environmental factors filtering plants based on their traits.
   In fact, both negative and positive plant-­to-­plant interactions are      However, plant-­to-­plant interactions enhancing environmental
frequently mediated by a range of environmental factors that inter-        heterogeneity would not necessarily lead to environmentally driven
acting plants modify at fine spatial and/or temporal scales. For in-       trait divergence if the factors involved do not interact in their fil-
stance, competing plants remove resources that then become more            tering effects. In the absence of factor interaction, environmental

heterogeneity caused by such feedback-­generated hidden factors                of SLA on grazing intensity is positive, communities exhibiting higher
would produce within-­community trait variation unrelated to the               SLA tend to experience more intensive grazing (Caram et al., 2023),
measured factor, as depicted in Figures 1a and 5. Interactions be-             creating heterogeneity in grazing intensity, which subsequently
tween ecological factors seem to be the rule in nature (e.g. Catford           influences the interaction. Consequently, the expected CWM for
et al., 2022; Spake et al., 2023). Therefore, this explanation for en-         SLA becomes more variable with higher soil fertility. Additionally,
vironmentally driven trait divergence appears to be more general-              the positive effect of SLA on grazing intensity negatively impacts
izable than considering plant competition and facilitation (Stubbs &           plant height at the community level. However, these effects are not
Wilson, 2004; Wilson, 2007; de Bello et al., 2021) or coexistence              entirely deterministic and depend on how critical the traits are for
theory predictions (Chesson, 2000; Mayfield & Levine, 2010;                    the fitness of each species in the species pool (as seen in Figure 3b)
Hillerislambers et al., 2012; Godoy et al., 2017; de Bello et al., 2021).      and on the stochasticity inherent in the simulation model. These
Although the mechanism of limiting similarity, where coexisting spe-           hypothetical indirect effects of soil fertility on plant height, via the
cies avoid competition through niche differentiation, can be linked to         feedback mechanism (Figure 5c), may explain the apparent inflated
feedback-­generated environmental heterogeneity and interpreted                Type I error, that is, the unexpectedly high frequency of significant
as environmental filtering taking place at a very fine grain (see, e.g.        beta trait divergence for trait t2 (Figure 5a). Even in the absence of
Carboni et al., 2014), it is unlikely that environmentally driven trait        an explicit interaction between soil fertility and grazing on plant
divergence, as defined here, would arise by limiting similarity alone          height built into the simulation model, the indirect effect via soil
in the absence of interaction between filtering factors.                       fertility → SLA → grazing → height may mimic the interaction effect,
    The results with simulated data provide valuable insights into the         thus generating beta trait divergence for height in these simulations.
reliability of detecting alpha and beta trait divergence using correla-           The analysis of the real grassland vegetation data in this study
tions r(RE) and r(VE), respectively, along with the proposed permu-            suggests that trait divergence can be generated by the interaction
tation test under the null model that assumes traits are unrelated             between heterogeneous vegetation cover and soil variables. In other
to the species composition. Additionally, a second-­order polynomial           words, the effect of vegetation cover heterogeneity on CWM SLA
regression should be employed to determine the residuals V needed              depends on the level of soil variables, and vice versa. Furthermore,
for measuring beta trait divergence by the correlation r(VE). This             some ecological factors driving community assembly may be spa-
approach reduces the risk of inflated Type I error when CWM trait              tially nested, such as in the analysed data. This means that what ap-
values exhibit non-­linear responses to environmental factors.                 pears as alpha trait divergence at one spatial scale (grain) could be
    Furthermore, analogous to results of Pillar et al. (2021), the             identified as beta trait divergence at a finer scale, indicating that the
simulated data revealed a suppression effect in which the role of              alpha and beta dimensions of trait divergence are interconnected.
the two-­f actor interaction in generating trait divergence was sup-           Beta trait divergence, as measured in this study, appears to be a
pressed by the stronger effect of the main factor. Conversely, such            more widespread pattern than previously acknowledged and could
interference between factors and between traits may have influ-                likely be detected in analyses conducted at global (e.g., Bruelheide
enced the Type I error. This suggests that these interferences arise           et al., 2018) and regional/local scales (e.g., Laughlin et al., 2018).
from how individual traits within the same plants are selected by                 In conclusion, the evidence strongly suggests that environmen-
different factors and their interactions, which deserves further               tally driven trait divergence in community assembly arises due to
investigation. The assembly process selects whole individuals                  factors that interact in the selection of individuals based on their
based on traits that cannot be disentangled, and in reality these              traits. If these factors are spatially nested, potentially driven by
traits are often non-­independent and coordinated with each other,             feedback during community development, such trait divergence
with relations restricted by trade-­offs (Díaz et al., 2016; Joswig            can be observed at either the beta or alpha dimensions, depending
et al., 2022). Thus, an unsuitable trait value can limit an individual         on the scale of the community units in such a heterogeneous envi-
plant's success in a community site, although plants vary in terms             ronment. However, it is essential to emphasize that such trait diver-
of how critical a trait is in this process. In relation to this, it is also    gence, whether at the beta or alpha level, is not solely a consequence
worth further investigating the role of feedback effects on com-               of environmental heterogeneity. Instead, it results from the interac-
munity assembly, as represented in the more complex simulated                  tive effects of various factors occurring in a heterogeneous environ-
scenario B.                                                                    ment, leading to fine-­scale trait variations among community sites.
    To enhance understanding, factors and traits in scenario B can
be translated into potential real counterparts, representing a plausi-         AC K N OW L E D G E M E N T S
ble path model for grassland communities under grazing (Figure 5c).            This work was presented as a keynote talk related to my induc-
In this hypothetical model, soil fertility exerts an expected posi-            tion as an honorary member of the IAVS during the 64th IAVS
tive effect on SLA, and grazing intensity a negative effect on plant           Symposium in Madrid, 2022, for which I thank the IAVS for the
height. Furthermore, when interaction is involved, the effect of soil          honour and invitation. I express my special gratitude to Luciana
